exp_L1_00.label: Diagnostic Accuracy & Trust
exp_L1_00.description: Concerns about whether AI tools actually help us make better calls—or just add noise and doubt.

exp_L2_00_00.label: False Positives/Negatives
exp_L2_00_00.description: AI misses things it shouldn’t or flags normal findings as suspicious, which messes with our confidence.

exp_leaf_00_00_00.label: Too many false alarms on benign nodules
exp_leaf_00_00_00.description: The AI keeps lighting up every tiny lung nodule like it’s cancer—even the ones we know are stable granulomas from old TB. It’s exhausting to keep overriding it.

exp_leaf_00_00_01.label: Misses subtle fractures in trauma cases
exp_leaf_00_00_01.description: In busy ER shifts, the AI sometimes glosses over hairline fractures—especially in wrist or rib X-rays—because they’re not textbook-perfect. We end up catching what the algorithm missed.

exp_leaf_00_00_02.label: Overlooks early signs of disease progression
exp_leaf_00_00_02.description: When comparing prior studies, the AI often fails to notice that a lesion grew by just 2mm—but that tiny change is clinically huge for us.

exp_leaf_00_00_03.label: Can't distinguish artifact from real pathology
exp_leaf_00_00_03.description: Metal streaks, motion blur, or even clothing folds get flagged as masses or bleeds. I spend more time debunking AI than diagnosing.

exp_L2_00_01.label: Black Box Reliability
exp_L2_00_01.description: We don’t know why the AI says what it says—and that makes it hard to trust or explain to patients.

exp_leaf_00_01_00.label: No explanation for why something was flagged
exp_leaf_00_01_00.description: It just highlights an area and says 'suspicious'—but gives zero reasoning. Was it texture? Shape? Location? Without that, I can’t validate or learn from it.

exp_leaf_00_01_01.label: Inconsistent behavior across similar cases
exp_leaf_00_01_01.description: Two nearly identical mammograms—one gets a high-risk flag, the other gets cleared. There’s no logic I can see, and that inconsistency kills trust fast.

exp_leaf_00_01_02.label: Feels like it’s guessing based on irrelevant features
exp_leaf_00_01_02.description: Sometimes I swear the AI is latching onto scanner brand or patient positioning—not actual anatomy. Like it learned to spot GE scanners instead of tumors.

exp_leaf_00_01_03.label: Hard to tell if it’s helping or hurting
exp_leaf_00_01_03.description: Even when the final diagnosis is right, I’m never sure if I got there despite the AI or because of it. That uncertainty lingers.
aliases: was it me or the machine?

exp_L2_00_02.label: Context Blindness
exp_L2_00_02.description: AI doesn’t know the patient story—it just sees pixels, not people.

exp_leaf_00_02_00.label: Ignores clinical history that changes interpretation
exp_leaf_00_02_00.description: If a patient has known sarcoidosis, those bilateral hilar nodes aren’t cancer—but the AI doesn’t care. It treats every scan like it’s from a healthy screening cohort.

exp_leaf_00_02_01.label: Can’t adjust for atypical presentations
exp_leaf_00_02_01.description: Immunocompromised patients show weird infection patterns—the AI trained on typical cases just shrugs or mislabels them as neoplasm.

exp_leaf_00_02_02.label: Misses when imaging quality is too poor to interpret
exp_leaf_00_02_02.description: The AI will confidently call a ‘negative’ on a motion-blurred abdominal CT—but any radiologist would say ‘non-diagnostic, repeat needed.’ It doesn’t know its own limits.

exp_leaf_00_02_03.label: Doesn’t understand urgency vs. routine
exp_leaf_00_02_03.description: In a code stroke, every second counts—but the AI processes the same way whether it’s an emergency or a follow-up. It lacks triage awareness.

exp_L1_01.label: Workflow Integration & Efficiency
exp_L1_01.description: How AI fits—or doesn’t—into our already overloaded daily grind.

exp_L2_01_00.label: Alert Fatigue
exp_L2_01_00.description: Too many pop-ups, banners, and suggestions drown out what actually matters.

exp_leaf_01_00_00.label: Constant interruptions during dictation
exp_leaf_01_00_00.description: Just as I’m describing a finding, a red banner pops up saying ‘possible PE’—even though I’ve already ruled it out. It breaks my train of thought.

exp_leaf_01_00_01.label: Low-value suggestions clutter the interface
exp_leaf_01_00_01.description: It suggests ‘consider lymphadenopathy’ on every chest CT—even when nodes are clearly normal. After a while, you start ignoring everything.

exp_leaf_01_00_02.label: Forced acknowledgment slows me down
exp_leaf_01_00_02.description: I have to click ‘dismiss’ or ‘accept’ on every AI note, even the dumb ones. In a high-volume shift, those seconds add up to real delays.

exp_leaf_01_00_03.label: Hard to silence without missing real alerts
exp_leaf_01_00_03.description: If I mute the AI to focus, I worry I’ll miss the one time it actually caught something important. It’s a lose-lose.

exp_L2_01_01.label: Tool Fragmentation
exp_L2_01_01.description: AI feels bolted on, not built in—and now I’m juggling five different windows.

exp_leaf_01_01_00.label: Separate login or tab for AI results
exp_leaf_01_01_00.description: I have to alt-tab to another browser window just to see what the AI thinks. Meanwhile, my PACS hangs. It’s clunky and error-prone.

exp_leaf_01_01_01.label: No native integration with voice recognition
exp_leaf_01_01_01.description: Dragon doesn’t talk to the AI plugin, so I can’t say ‘accept AI finding’—I have to mouse-click. Feels like tech from 2005.

exp_leaf_01_01_02.label: Results don’t auto-populate into report templates
exp_leaf_01_01_02.description: The AI finds three liver lesions, but I still have to type them all out. If it’s not saving me keystrokes, what’s the point?

exp_leaf_01_01_03.label: Different AIs for different modalities = cognitive overhead
exp_leaf_01_01_03.description: One vendor for mammo, another for neuro, another for body—each with its own UI quirks. My brain can’t context-switch that fast.

exp_L2_01_02.label: Time Pressure vs. Verification Burden
exp_L2_01_02.description: AI is supposed to save time—but often adds steps I didn’t ask for.

exp_leaf_01_02_00.label: Now I have to double-check the AI’s work too
exp_leaf_01_02_00.description: Instead of just reading the case, I’m auditing the algorithm. That’s extra mental labor with no extra pay or time.

exp_leaf_01_02_01.label: Slows me down on straightforward cases
exp_leaf_01_02_01.description: On a clean ankle X-ray, the AI still takes 10 seconds to load and highlight ‘no fracture’—when I could’ve signed it off in 5.

exp_leaf_01_02_02.label: Hard to skip AI when I’m confident
exp_leaf_01_02_02.description: There’s no ‘I got this’ button. Even when I know exactly what’s going on, the system forces me through the AI step.

exp_leaf_01_02_03.label: Uncertainty about when to trust vs. verify
exp_leaf_01_02_03.description: Is this a case where the AI usually nails it? Or one where it’s historically flaky? I don’t have a mental map—I just wing it.
aliases: when to lean in vs. lean out

exp_L1_02.label: Legal, Ethical & Accountability
exp_L1_02.description: Who’s liable when AI misses something—or convinces me to miss it?

exp_L2_02_00.label: Liability Ambiguity
exp_L2_02_00.description: If the AI leads me astray, am I still 100% on the hook?

exp_leaf_02_00_00.label: No clear standard for AI-assisted reads
exp_leaf_02_00_00.description: Courts and boards haven’t said whether using AI changes the standard of care. Am I negligent if I ignore it? Or if I follow it blindly?

exp_leaf_02_00_01.label: Fear of being second-guessed for overriding AI
exp_leaf_02_00_01.description: If I dismiss an AI alert and later it turns out to be real, the plaintiff’s lawyer will say ‘the computer saw it—you didn’t.’ Even if the AI was wrong 90% of the time.

exp_leaf_02_00_02.label: Vendor won’t share model details for defense
exp_leaf_02_00_02.description: If sued, I can’t subpoena how the AI works—it’s all trade secret. So I can’t prove it was flawed; I just look careless.

exp_leaf_02_00_03.label: Documentation burden increases with AI use
exp_leaf_02_00_03.description: Now I have to write ‘AI suggested X, but I interpreted as Y because…’ in every report. More words, more time, more exposure.

exp_L2_02_01.label: Informed Consent & Patient Communication
exp_L2_02_01.description: Do patients know AI is involved? Should they?

exp_leaf_02_01_00.label: Patients assume AI = infallible
exp_leaf_02_01_00.description: When I tell someone their scan was ‘reviewed by AI,’ they think it’s foolproof. Then they’re shocked when we find something later. Managing expectations is tough.

exp_leaf_02_01_01.label: No guidance on disclosing AI use
exp_leaf_02_01_01.description: Should I mention AI in the consent form? In the report? Nobody’s told us. So we’re all making it up as we go.

exp_leaf_02_01_02.label: Hard to explain AI errors to patients
exp_leaf_02_01_02.description: ‘The computer missed it’ sounds unprofessional. But saying ‘I missed it’ erodes trust. There’s no good script for this conversation.

exp_leaf_02_01_03.label: Ethical discomfort with opaque decision aids
exp_leaf_02_01_03.description: I feel uneasy using a tool that affects someone’s cancer diagnosis—but that I can’t interrogate or validate myself.

exp_L2_02_02.label: Bias & Equity Concerns
exp_L2_02_02.description: Worries that AI might worsen disparities—especially if trained on non-diverse data.

exp_leaf_02_02_00.label: Poor performance on underrepresented populations
exp_leaf_02_02_00.description: The AI was trained mostly on Caucasian males—it struggles with denser breasts in younger Black women or different bone densities in Asian elders.

exp_leaf_02_02_01.label: Reinforces existing diagnostic biases
exp_leaf_02_02_01.description: If historical reads undercalled certain conditions in women or minorities, the AI learns to do the same—and amplifies it at scale.

exp_leaf_02_02_02.label: Hard to audit for fairness without ground truth
exp_leaf_02_02_02.description: We don’t always know the real diagnosis—so how do I check if the AI is fair? Autopsy rates are near zero these days.

exp_leaf_02_02_03.label: Unequal access to high-quality AI tools
exp_leaf_02_02_03.description: Academic centers get the latest models; rural clinics get outdated or no AI. That could widen the care gap even more.

exp_L1_03.label: Training, Education & Skill Erosion
exp_L1_03.description: How AI changes how we learn—and whether it’s making us worse doctors over time.

exp_L2_03_00.label: Resident Overreliance
exp_L2_03_00.description: Trainees are leaning on AI too early, before they’ve built foundational pattern recognition.

exp_leaf_03_00_00.label: Residents trust AI over their own eyes
exp_leaf_03_00_00.description: I’ll ask a resident what they see, and they’ll say ‘the AI says negative’—without even looking properly. Their visual diagnostic muscles are atrophying.

exp_leaf_03_00_01.label: Less time spent on differential reasoning
exp_leaf_03_00_01.description: Why brainstorm possibilities when the AI gives you one answer? Residents aren’t practicing the ‘what else could this be?’ mindset anymore.

exp_leaf_03_00_02.label: Hard to teach uncertainty when AI is binary
exp_leaf_03_00_02.description: AI says ‘positive/negative’—but real radiology lives in the gray zone. Trainees struggle with probabilistic thinking now.

exp_leaf_03_00_03.label: Residents don’t learn from AI mistakes
exp_leaf_03_00_03.description: When AI errs, residents often don’t notice—or if they do, they don’t internalize why. The learning loop is broken.

exp_L2_03_01.label: Continuing Education Gaps
exp_L2_03_01.description: We’re expected to use AI—but nobody taught us how to evaluate or integrate it wisely.

exp_leaf_03_01_00.label: No training on AI limitations or failure modes
exp_leaf_03_01_00.description: Vendors demo the wins, not the losses. We learn the hard way—usually after a near-miss.

exp_leaf_03_01_01.label: Don’t know how to assess new AI tools critically
exp_leaf_03_01_01.description: A rep shows me an AUC of 0.95—but is that on clean research data or messy real-world scans? I lack the stats literacy to push back.

exp_leaf_03_01_02.label: Hard to stay updated on AI performance drift
exp_leaf_03_01_02.description: The model updates silently, and suddenly it’s behaving differently. There’s no changelog or re-validation notice.

exp_leaf_03_01_03.label: Unclear how to blend AI input with expert judgment
exp_leaf_03_01_03.description: Am I supposed to treat AI like a junior resident? A second reader? A statistical hint? Nobody’s given us a mental model for this partnership.

exp_L2_03_02.label: Skill Atrophy in Experts
exp_L2_03_02.description: Even seasoned radiologists worry their skills are dulling from overuse of AI crutches.

exp_leaf_03_02_00.label: Less practice detecting subtle findings
exp_leaf_03_02_00.description: If AI always catches the obvious stuff, I’m not exercising my eye for the faint, tricky signs anymore. It’s like losing night vision.

exp_leaf_03_02_01.label: Reduced motivation to correlate across modalities
exp_leaf_03_02_01.description: Why dig into old MRI or PET scans when the AI gives a quick answer on the current CT? My integrative thinking is getting lazy.

exp_leaf_03_02_02.label: Overconfidence in routine cases
exp_leaf_03_02_02.description: When AI clears 100 chest X-rays in a row, I start autopiloting—and then miss the one weird case that breaks the pattern.

exp_leaf_03_02_03.label: Less engagement with negative cases
exp_leaf_03_02_03.description: AI handles the ‘boring’ negatives, so I’m only deeply analyzing positives. But learning happens in the negatives too—what’s normal, what’s variant.

exp_L1_04.label: Data, Infrastructure & Operational Realities
exp_L1_04.description: The gritty, behind-the-scenes headaches that make or break AI adoption.

exp_L2_04_00.label: Data Quality & Compatibility
exp_L2_04_00.description: AI needs clean, consistent data—but real-world imaging is messy and fragmented.

exp_leaf_04_00_00.label: AI fails on non-standard protocols
exp_leaf_04_00_00.description: If a referring clinic uses weird slice thickness or non-DICOM formats, the AI either crashes or spits nonsense. Real life isn’t a curated dataset.

exp_leaf_04_00_01.label: Prior studies often missing or mislinked
exp_leaf_04_00_01.description: AI claims to do longitudinal analysis—but half the time, prior scans aren’t in the system or are filed under the wrong accession number.

exp_leaf_04_00_02.label: Inconsistent labeling across institutions
exp_leaf_04_00_02.description: What one hospital calls ‘indeterminate’ another calls ‘suspicious.’ AI trained on mixed labels gets confused—and so do we.

exp_leaf_04_00_03.label: Hard to validate AI on local data
exp_leaf_04_00_03.description: Our patient mix is different—more diabetics, more transplants—but we can’t easily test if the AI works here without a full validation study.

exp_L2_04_01.label: IT & Integration Burden
exp_L2_04_01.description: Getting AI to run smoothly requires constant tech babysitting most departments can’t afford.

exp_leaf_04_01_00.label: Frequent downtime during system updates
exp_leaf_04_01_00.description: Every time PACS updates, the AI plugin breaks for a day. We’re left scrambling without it—even if we’d rather not use it, workflows depend on it now.

exp_leaf_04_01_01.label: Slow inference on older hardware
exp_leaf_04_01_01.description: Our reading stations are 3 years old—AI takes 30 seconds to process a CT. In a stat setting, that’s unusable.

exp_leaf_04_01_02.label: No centralized management console
exp_leaf_04_01_02.description: Each AI tool has its own admin panel. Turning one off or updating settings means logging into five different portals.

exp_leaf_04_01_03.label: Bandwidth issues with cloud-based AI
exp_leaf_04_01_03.description: Uploading full studies to the cloud for analysis eats our network—slowing down everything else during peak hours.

exp_L2_04_02.label: Economic & Reimbursement Pressures
exp_L2_04_02.description: Nobody’s paying for the extra work AI creates—and vendors charge a fortune.

exp_leaf_04_02_00.label: No reimbursement for AI validation time
exp_leaf_04_02_00.description: I spend 15 minutes per complex case verifying AI output—but insurers don’t recognize that as billable work. It’s pure cost.

exp_leaf_04_02_01.label: High licensing costs with unclear ROI
exp_leaf_04_02_01.description: We pay $100K/year per AI module—but can’t prove it saves money or improves outcomes enough to justify it.

exp_leaf_04_02_02.label: Pressure to adopt AI for marketing, not medicine
exp_leaf_04_02_02.description: Admin wants to advertise ‘AI-powered radiology’ to attract patients—even though the tool barely helps clinically. It’s tech theater.

exp_leaf_04_02_03.label: Fear that AI will be used to justify staffing cuts
exp_leaf_04_02_03.description: If AI ‘handles’ 30% of cases, will they reduce our FTEs? Productivity gains might backfire on job security.

res_L1_00.label: Model Interpretability & Trust
res_L1_00.description: Understanding how domain experts assess, interpret, and build trust in AI-generated predictions from virtual cell models.

res_L2_00_00.label: Reliability Assessment
res_L2_00_00.description: How experts judge whether a virtual cell model is trustworthy or reliable.

res_leaf_00_00_00.label: Identify metrics used to evaluate model reliability
res_leaf_00_00_00.description: Determine which quantitative or qualitative metrics (e.g., accuracy, biological plausibility, reproducibility) experts rely on when assessing virtual cell models like CellForge.

res_leaf_00_00_01.label: Understand contextual factors influencing trust
res_leaf_00_00_01.description: Explore non-metric factors (e.g., alignment with prior knowledge, peer validation) that shape expert confidence in AI predictions.

res_leaf_00_00_02.label: Assess familiarity as a proxy for trust
res_leaf_00_00_02.description: Investigate whether prior exposure to tools like CellForge correlates with perceived reliability or willingness to adopt.

res_L2_00_01.label: Explainability Needs
res_L2_00_01.description: What kinds of explanations do experts need to understand AI predictions about gene perturbations?

res_leaf_00_01_00.label: Map desired explanation types to biological reasoning tasks
res_leaf_00_01_00.description: Link specific explanation needs (e.g., causal pathways, confidence levels) to concrete research goals like identifying disease-related genes or drug targets.

res_leaf_00_01_01.label: Evaluate challenges in understanding AI predictions
res_leaf_00_01_01.description: Identify specific aspects of AI output that are confusing or misleading during gene perturbation analysis.

res_leaf_00_01_02.label: Assess utility of XAI techniques for biological insight
res_leaf_00_01_02.description: Determine which explainable AI methods (e.g., saliency maps, rule extraction) would meaningfully support hypothesis generation in genetic research.

res_L1_01.label: Domain Workflow Integration
res_L1_01.description: How AI-assisted diagnosis and virtual cell models fit into existing scientific workflows and experimental practices.

res_L2_01_00.label: Current Experimental Practices
res_L2_01_00.description: Characterize manual approaches to genetic perturbation experiments to identify integration points for AI tools.

res_leaf_01_00_00.label: Document standard manual workflow for gene perturbation
res_leaf_01_00_00.description: Capture step-by-step procedures, decision points, and data sources used in traditional wet-lab or computational genetic perturbation studies.

res_leaf_01_00_01.label: Identify bottlenecks suitable for AI augmentation
res_leaf_01_00_01.description: Pinpoint time-consuming, error-prone, or hypothesis-limiting stages where virtual cell models could add value.

res_leaf_01_00_02.label: Understand validation practices in manual workflows
res_leaf_01_00_02.description: Learn how experts verify results without AI to inform how AI outputs should be validated or cross-checked.

res_L2_01_01.label: AI Tool Adoption Barriers
res_L2_01_01.description: Barriers preventing effective use of AI-based gene prediction models in real-world research settings.

res_leaf_01_01_00.label: Catalog challenges in using AI gene prediction models
res_leaf_01_01_00.description: Gather reported difficulties such as data format mismatches, lack of control, or poor integration with lab systems.

res_leaf_01_01_01.label: Assess perceived risk of AI errors in diagnosis contexts
res_leaf_01_01_01.description: Explore concerns about false positives/negatives in AI predictions and their downstream impact on research or clinical decisions.

res_leaf_01_01_02.label: Identify prerequisites for adoption
res_leaf_01_01_02.description: Determine what features, evidence, or training would be needed for experts to incorporate AI models into routine practice.

res_L1_02.label: Knowledge Discovery & Scientific Reasoning
res_L1_02.description: How experts aim to generate new biological insights using virtual cell models and what knowledge gaps they seek to fill.

res_L2_02_00.label: Targeted Biological Questions
res_L2_02_00.description: The specific scientific problems experts hope to solve using AI-assisted perturbation models.

res_leaf_02_00_00.label: Elicit key research questions driving model use
res_leaf_02_00_00.description: Capture goals such as identifying causal gene networks, predicting phenotypic outcomes, or discovering therapeutic targets.

res_leaf_02_00_01.label: Differentiate between predictive and mechanistic understanding needs
res_leaf_02_00_01.description: Clarify whether experts prioritize accurate predictions or interpretable mechanisms underlying gene interactions.

res_leaf_02_00_02.label: Map desired outputs to disease or trait contexts
res_leaf_02_00_02.description: Understand how the relevance of model outputs varies by disease area or cellular phenotype of interest.

res_L2_02_01.label: Supporting Evidence & External Knowledge
res_L2_02_01.description: What external information sources experts want integrated to validate or enrich AI predictions.

res_leaf_02_01_00.label: Identify complementary data sources for interpretation
res_leaf_02_01_00.description: Determine which external resources (e.g., literature, pathway databases, prior experiments) experts would use to contextualize AI results.

res_leaf_02_01_01.label: Assess role of literature in verifying AI predictions
res_leaf_02_01_01.description: Explore how experts use published findings to confirm or challenge model outputs about gene effects.

res_leaf_02_01_02.label: Evaluate need for interactive knowledge linking
res_leaf_02_01_02.description: Determine if dynamic linking between model outputs and biological knowledge bases would improve usability and insight.

res_L1_03.label: User Experience & Interface Design
res_L1_03.description: Design considerations for interfaces that support effective interaction with explainable AI in medical genomics.

res_L2_03_00.label: Explanation Presentation Preferences
res_L2_03_00.description: How experts prefer explanations to be visualized or communicated within their analytical environment.

res_leaf_03_00_00.label: Evaluate effectiveness of proposed XAI methods
res_leaf_03_00_00.description: Assess which explanation formats (from ExplainableArtificialIntelligence_XAI.pptx) align with expert cognitive styles and tasks.

res_leaf_03_00_01.label: Determine granularity of desired explanations
res_leaf_03_00_01.description: Understand whether experts need gene-level, pathway-level, or system-level explanations for perturbation effects.

res_leaf_03_00_02.label: Identify interaction needs for exploring explanations
res_leaf_03_00_02.description: Discover desired capabilities such as drilling down into mechanisms, comparing perturbations, or simulating interventions.

res_L2_03_01.label: Cognitive & Collaborative Load
res_L2_03_01.description: How AI tools impact mental effort and team dynamics in genomic research.

res_leaf_03_01_00.label: Measure perceived cognitive burden of interpreting AI outputs
res_leaf_03_01_00.description: Assess whether current or proposed AI interfaces increase or reduce mental effort during analysis.

res_leaf_03_01_01.label: Understand collaborative implications of AI use
res_leaf_03_01_01.description: Explore how AI predictions are discussed, challenged, or shared among interdisciplinary teams (e.g., biologists, clinicians, data scientists).

aligned_path_researcher: Identify metrics used to evaluate model reliability

conflict.expert_branch: Inconsistent behavior across similar cases
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify metrics used to evaluate model reliability

analogy.source_concept: Feels like it’s guessing based on irrelevant features

structural_mapping.inputs: Nearly identical mammograms with subtle, clinically irrelevant differences (e.g., scanner brand or positioning)
structural_mapping.logic: AI assigns risk based on spurious correlations rather than consistent pathological signals
structural_mapping.outputs: Divergent diagnostic flags that appear arbitrary to the clinician

analogy.explanation: Just as the AI appears to latch onto scanner artifacts instead of tumors, reliability metrics like reproducibility measure whether the model responds consistently to clinically meaningful inputs—not noise. If a model fails this, its reliability metrics will reflect instability, mirroring the expert’s observation of inconsistency across similar cases.

assistance_payload.scenario: Two nearly identical mammograms differ only in background noise, yet one is flagged high-risk and the other cleared. The researcher’s reliability metrics would quantify this inconsistency via reproducibility scores across perturbed inputs. But if those metrics are averaged over many cases, they may mask the very local instability that erodes the expert’s trust in individual decisions.

aligned_path_expert: Ignores clinical history that changes interpretation
aligned_path_researcher: Identify complementary data sources for interpretation

conflict.expert_branch: Ignores clinical history that changes interpretation
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify complementary data sources for interpretation

assistance_payload.validate_focus: Your focus on ensuring AI respects critical patient-specific clinical history—like known sarcoidosis altering the meaning of imaging findings—is essential for safe, accurate diagnosis in real-world practice.

pivot.limitation: Relying solely on existing clinical history in isolation may miss nuanced or emerging connections that could further refine interpretation, especially when prior diagnoses interact with rare conditions or novel presentations.
pivot.research_goal: Identify complementary data sources for interpretation, such as curated medical literature or disease pathway databases, to enrich AI reasoning with expert-vetted contextual knowledge beyond the immediate chart.
pivot.compelling_reason: This approach doesn’t replace your clinical judgment—it arms you with a broader, evidence-based context so the AI’s suggestions align not just with the patient’s history, but also with the latest collective medical understanding, reducing diagnostic surprises.
pivot.coarse_scenario: On a typical morning, you review an AI-highlighted lung scan. Instead of flagging benign nodes as suspicious, the system now surfaces relevant literature and similar case patterns anchored to the patient’s known sarcoidosis, letting you confirm benignity confidently and spend more time discussing care—not clarifying false alarms.

aligned_path_expert: No clear standard for AI-assisted reads

conflict.expert_branch: No clear standard for AI-assisted reads
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify prerequisites for adoption

assistance_payload.validate_focus: Your focus on the legal and ethical uncertainty around AI-assisted reads is critically important—clinicians need clear guidance on whether using or ignoring AI outputs constitutes negligence under current standards of care.

pivot.limitation: Relying solely on reactive legal interpretations may leave clinicians without proactive frameworks to confidently integrate AI in ways that demonstrably uphold or exceed the standard of care.
pivot.research_goal: Identify prerequisites for adoption—such as required evidence, training, or system features—that would enable safe, reliable, and defensible use of AI in clinical workflows.
pivot.compelling_reason: By defining these prerequisites, we create a foundation that not only supports smoother adoption but also strengthens your legal and professional position by showing due diligence in how AI tools are selected, validated, and applied.
pivot.coarse_scenario: In a typical day, a radiologist reviews an AI-assisted scan with confidence, knowing the tool was adopted based on clear, field-endorsed criteria. When questioned later, they can point to standardized protocols and validated performance benchmarks that align with—and even reinforce—the prevailing standard of care.

conflict.expert_branch: Patients assume AI = infallible
conflict.researcher_branch: Understand contextual factors influencing trust

assistance_payload.validate_focus: Your focus on managing patient expectations around AI’s fallibility is crucial—it directly impacts trust, compliance, and emotional well-being during diagnosis.

pivot.limitation: Relying solely on verbal disclaimers may not effectively recalibrate patient trust if we don’t understand what actually shapes their perception of AI reliability in the first place.
pivot.research_goal: Understand contextual factors influencing trust—such as how patients interpret AI roles based on communication style, clinician endorsement, or prior health experiences.
pivot.compelling_reason: By uncovering these hidden drivers, you’ll gain actionable insights to tailor your explanations in ways that genuinely align patient expectations with AI’s real-world capabilities—reducing shock, blame, and legal risk.
pivot.coarse_scenario: A patient receives an AI-assisted result, but because the clinician frames it using language calibrated to that patient’s trust triggers—like referencing peer-reviewed use or explaining uncertainty in relatable terms—they respond with informed curiosity instead of blind faith.
